text.title: Batch API Lifecycle and Error Handling
text.summary: Rules for submitting, polling, and consuming Batch API results, including error detection and the cost model.

rule.statement: Use custom_id values that map directly to database primary keys or job IDs.
rule.rationale: Results arrive out of order. Without a custom_id that maps to your data model, matching results back to inputs requires additional joins or lookups.

rule.statement: Group batch requests by the same model and max_tokens to simplify result processing.
rule.rationale: Mixing models in one batch is allowed but produces heterogeneous result structures. Uniform batches simplify downstream parsing.

rule.statement: Poll at intervals of at least 60 seconds; do not use a tight loop.
rule.rationale: Batch processing takes minutes to hours. Tight polling wastes API quota and adds no speed benefit.

rule.statement: Check processing_status == "ended" before accessing results; do not assume completion on first poll.
rule.rationale: The batch may still be processing. Accessing results before "ended" returns an empty or partial result set.

rule.statement: Detect result.result.type == "errored" and resubmit failed items explicitly; Batch API does not retry automatically.
rule.rationale: Errored requests are included in the results file with type "errored". They are not retried. Your code must detect and resubmit them.

section.title: Cost Model
section.p: Batch API provides 50% cost reduction versus synchronous calls. Maximum latency is 24 hours. Use only for workloads where 24-hour SLA is acceptable: content generation, offline enrichment, evaluation suites, nightly analysis.

example.note: Errored results are included in the return list with an "error" key for upstream handling.

antipattern.description: Using Batch API for user-facing real-time responses.
antipattern.reason: Batch API can take up to 24 hours. Any user-facing flow requiring a response within seconds must use synchronous Messages API instead.
